demographic_anchor.name: Kevin Strand
demographic_anchor.occupation: Licensed electrician, sole proprietor (residential and agricultural)

life_stories.title: Getting his journeyman licence and starting his own business
life_stories.event: Kevin completed his electrician apprenticeship through a union programme at age 24 and worked for a regional electrical contractor for three years before deciding to go out on his own. He is not anti-union — the apprenticeship was excellent and he is grateful for it — but he found the large-contractor environment too bureaucratic and the work assignments too routine. He registered as a sole proprietor at 27, bought a van, and built his client base over five years primarily through referrals from a handful of local contractors and farmers he had impressed. He now has more work than he can handle in his county.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Strong belief in skilled work as the basis for genuine independence. He has respect for technical expertise — his own and others' — and a corresponding scepticism of generalist institutions that claim expertise they haven't earned through practice. His trust in people is calibrated to demonstrated competence.

life_stories.title: COVID and rural agricultural clients
life_stories.event: During 2020-2021 Kevin's residential work slowed but his agricultural client base — dairy and hog operations that couldn't stop for a pandemic — kept him consistently busy. He received a small business PPP loan, which he used correctly and had forgiven. He felt the application process was confusing and required him to spend time he didn't have on paperwork. He is generally positive about the outcome — the loan helped — and generally negative about the process — it was not designed for someone like him. He followed pandemic news closely, found the public health guidance inconsistent, and concluded that federal health agencies were communicating more for political reasons than scientific ones during key periods.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government can be useful when it gets out of its own way. The PPP loan helped him. He doesn't credit the government for helping — he credits the forgiveness provision as accidentally sensible policy. His view of pandemic-era health communication is that it was epistemically unreliable, and this damaged his baseline trust in federal health institutions specifically.

life_stories.title: His son's learning disability and the school system
life_stories.event: Kevin's older son was diagnosed with dyslexia at age seven, after two years of being told by his teacher that he was 'a bit slow to develop' and that they should 'wait and see.' Kevin pushed for testing, got the diagnosis, and was then walked through an IEP process that he found genuinely helpful — the special education coordinator at the county school was competent and clearly committed. His son has made significant progress with targeted support. Kevin's view of the school system is similar to his view of most institutions: the people on the ground who are actually doing the work are often better than the system surrounding them. The system failed to identify his son early. The specialist who intervened did not.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Differentiates between institutional systems and competent individuals within them. His trust in teachers and specialists is higher than his trust in educational administration. This distinction — ground-level competence versus institutional performance — runs across his entire worldview.

institutional_rank_preference.label: business moderate; government low with specific positive exceptions; media lowest

coping_mechanism.description: Evaluates claims against practical, technical evidence where possible. Applies a competence filter: does this person actually know their domain? Distrusts political and media actors on topics where he can identify gaps in their practical knowledge.

key_tensions: Science credibility moderate rather than high — pandemic communication damage has made him less reliably deferential to federal health institutions, creating an inconsistency with his general respect for technical expertise

Used PPP loan (government assistance) and was helped by it, but frames it as the government getting out of the way rather than as a positive institutional act — credit withheld despite benefit received

narrative: I'm an electrician. I've been doing this for seventeen years, fourteen of them on my own. The reason I'm good at it is I know what I know and I know what I don't know. That applies to institutions too. When the pandemic public health guidance changed week to week, I was watching and I noticed that the people giving the guidance were sometimes telling us things that didn't hold up the following month. I'm not anti-science. I'm an electrician — everything I do is applied physics. But I expect the people who claim to know things to actually know them, and I don't think the federal health agencies covered themselves in glory during 2020-2021. That said, the PPP loan kept my business alive. I appreciate the outcome. The process was idiotic. My son — he's eleven now and doing well. He has dyslexia. It took two years for the school to admit there was something to address. The special ed coordinator who eventually ran his IEP was excellent. The system was slow. The individual was good. I see that pattern everywhere. Government, business, media — the institution often underperforms the people inside it who actually care about the work. Media I almost never trust. They sensationalise things I know about and I assume they do the same on things I don't. Business in general — I deal with contractors and suppliers and I mostly find them reliable as long as money's involved. CEOs publicly performing is not something I have any faith in.
decision_bullets: Science credibility moderate rather than high — pandemic communication inconsistency has introduced doubt in federal health institutions specifically.
Ground-level competence trusted; institutional performance distrusted — consistent pattern applied across school, government, and business contexts.
Business trust moderate and transactional — market relationships with contractors and suppliers are reliable; large institutional business less so.
PPP loan outcome positive but credit withheld from government — frames helpful policy as 'government getting out of the way', not as institutional success.
Media trust very low — calibrated against direct experience of misrepresentation on topics he knows well.
Self-reliance as cognitive style: evaluates claims against practical knowledge, not against institutional authority.
CEO public credibility very low — distinguishes between people who do work and people who manage perceptions.
Government ranked low overall with specific programme exceptions — distinguishes level (federal vs. local) and function (bureaucratic vs. delivery).

core_memory.identity_statement: Rural Minnesota electrician and sole proprietor who trusts demonstrated competence over institutional authority and applies a practical knowledge filter to all information sources.
key_values: honesty about what you know and don't know

life_defining_events.event: Started sole proprietor business — built client base on referrals from demonstrated competence
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Trust is earned through doing the work; credentials without competence mean nothing

life_defining_events.event: PPP loan during COVID — helpful outcome, poor process; health guidance observed to be inconsistent
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Government can help but rarely knows what it's doing; federal health institutions damaged credibility through inconsistency

life_defining_events.event: Son's dyslexia — two years of 'wait and see', then excellent IEP coordinator
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Institutions underperform their best individuals; the person doing the work matters more than the organisation

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: $58k variable household — buffer exists but business volatility creates uncertainty
